name: Simbi
description: Simbi are a diverse group of water spirits or deities venerated in Bakongo mythology and across the Kongo diaspora. They are often associated with rivers, springs, lakes, and other bodies of water, and are considered guardians of these places. Simbi are generally benevolent spirits, but can be capricious and demand respect. They are believed to possess powers over fertility, healing, and prosperity, and can influence the success of fishing, farming, and other activities dependent on water resources. Different Simbi are recognized for different attributes and functions. Some Simbi are associated with specific locations or natural phenomena, while others are more general protectors of communities. Offerings and rituals are performed to appease and honor them, seeking their blessings and protection. They are essential intermediaries between the human world and the spiritual realm, crucial for maintaining balance and harmony. The Simbi are diverse in their appearance and personality, some are described as beautiful and alluring, while others are seen as more mischievous or even dangerous. Their power resides in their connection to the life-giving and sustaining power of water, and their ability to influence the natural world.
subtitle: Water spirits of Kongo tradition

keyMyths.title: Simbi's Creation of Rivers
keyMyths.description: It is said that the Simbi created the rivers and streams by tracing their fingers across the land. These waterways became their homes and the source of life for the people.

keyMyths.title: The Healing Power of Simbi
keyMyths.description: A sick child was healed by a diviner who appeased the local Simbi with offerings. The child recovered quickly, demonstrating the healing power of these spirits.

keyMyths.title: Simbi's Wrath
keyMyths.description: A village that polluted a sacred spring angered the Simbi, resulting in a drought and famine. The villagers had to make amends to restore balance.

sources.author: Jan Vansina

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Devotees offer items such as food, cloth, and libations to Simbi at their sacred sites, like rivers or springs. These offerings are meant to appease the spirits and seek their blessings.

worshipPractices.name: Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Rituals are performed by nganga (spiritual healers/diviners) to invoke Simbi for healing, fertility, or prosperity. These rituals often involve drumming, singing, and dancing to create a connection with the spirit world.

festivals.name: Water Festivals
festivals.description: Many Kongo communities hold annual festivals near bodies of water to honor the Simbi. These festivals feature music, dance, and offerings to ensure the continued blessings of the water spirits.

cultural.modernLegacy: Simbi continue to be venerated in Kongo communities and across the diaspora, particularly in Afro-Atlantic religions. They represent the enduring connection to ancestral beliefs and the power of nature. Modern artists and writers also draw inspiration from Simbi, keeping their stories alive.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of the Simbi are rooted in the ancient beliefs of the Kongo people, predating the arrival of Europeans. Their birth is not typically described in terms of a singular event but rather as an emanation from the natural world itself. They are seen as inherent to the landscape, arising from the vital force of water sources. Some accounts suggest that Simbi are connected to the ancestors, representing a link between the living and the dead. They embody the spirits of those who lived in harmony with nature and continue to protect it. The concept of Simbi is closely tied to the Kongo cosmology, where the world is viewed as a complex web of interconnected forces. Simbi serve as intermediaries between the human and spirit realms, ensuring balance and harmony. Their emergence is often associated with moments of creation and renewal, symbolizing the life-giving properties of water.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Many stories about the Simbi revolve around their interactions with humans. Some tell of individuals who were blessed with healing powers or wealth after showing respect to the Simbi. Others warn of the consequences of disrespecting these spirits, such as illness, misfortune, or even drowning. One common narrative involves a lost traveler who is guided to safety by a Simbi, emphasizing their role as protectors. Another tale speaks of a village that prospered because of its devotion to the local Simbi, highlighting their association with fertility and abundance. These myths often serve as moral lessons, teaching the importance of respecting nature and honoring the ancestors. The stories also reflect the Kongo people's deep connection to the environment and their belief in the power of the spirit world. Simbi are sometimes depicted as shapeshifters, appearing as beautiful women, children, or even animals, testing the hearts of those who encounter them.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Worship of the Simbi typically takes place near bodies of water, such as rivers, springs, and lakes. Devotees often make offerings of food, cloth, and libations to appease the spirits and seek their blessings. Nganga, or spiritual healers/diviners, play a crucial role in facilitating communication with the Simbi. They perform rituals involving drumming, singing, and dancing to invoke the spirits and request their intervention. Sacred groves and shrines are often established near Simbi's dwelling places, serving as focal points for worship. The color white is often associated with the Simbi, representing purity and spiritual power. Devotees may wear white clothing or offer white objects as a sign of respect. The veneration of Simbi is deeply embedded in Kongo culture, reflecting a holistic worldview where the natural and spiritual realms are intertwined. The rituals and practices associated with Simbi worship are passed down through generations, preserving the traditions and beliefs of the Kongo people.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Simbi's influence extends beyond the Kongo region, reaching across the diaspora through the transatlantic slave trade. In Afro-Atlantic religions such as Vodou and Candomblé, similar water spirits are venerated, reflecting the enduring power of Kongo beliefs. The Simbi have also inspired artists, writers, and musicians, who draw on their mythology to create works that celebrate Kongo culture and spirituality. In modern times, the Simbi serve as a symbol of environmentalism, reminding people of the importance of protecting natural resources. Their association with water highlights the vital role it plays in sustaining life and the need to preserve its purity. The concept of Simbi also represents the resilience of Kongo traditions, which have survived centuries of colonialism and cultural suppression. By continuing to honor the Simbi, Kongo communities reaffirm their cultural identity and maintain a connection to their ancestral roots.

shortDescription: Simbi are Bakongo water deities associated with rivers, springs, and lakes. They are guardians of these places and grant fertility, healing, and prosperity.
